annotations.label: Initial value problem
annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated `IVP` structure; an initial value problem is expressed by the pattern `α t₀ = y₀ ∧ ∀ t ∈ I, HasDerivAt α (f t (α t)) t` (or `HasDerivWithinAt` on a closed interval).

annotations.label: Lipschitz continuity (in the y-variable)
annotations.note: `LipschitzOnWith K (f t) s` captures Lipschitz continuity of `f` in `y` on a set `s` with constant `K` independent of `t`, exactly the form used in `IsPicardLindelof`.

annotations.label: Picard–Lindelöf theorem (local existence and uniqueness)
annotations.note: Existence is in `IsPicardLindelof.exists_eq_forall_mem_Icc_hasDerivWithinAt`; uniqueness on the same interval is supplied separately by `ODE_solution_unique` in `Mathlib/Analysis/ODE/Gronwall.lean` (via Grönwall).
annotations.proof_note: Mathlib factors the proof so existence (Banach fixed point) and uniqueness (Grönwall inequality) live in separate files, rather than deriving uniqueness from the single fixed point as the article does.

annotations.label: Reformulation as an integral equation

anchors.snippet: any solution to the differential equation must also satisfy

annotations.note: Mathlib produces a solution as a fixed point of the integral operator `ODE.picard`, i.e. `α t = x + ∫ τ in t₀..t, f τ (α τ)`, which is the article's integral-equation reformulation.

annotations.label: Banach fixed-point theorem
annotations.note: `ContractingWith.fixedPoint` produces the unique fixed point of a contraction on a complete metric space; existence is `fixedPoint_isFixedPt` and uniqueness is `fixedPoint_unique`.

annotations.label: Picard iteration
annotations.note: `ODE.picard f t₀ x₀ α t = x₀ + ∫ τ in t₀..t, f τ (α τ)` is exactly the Picard operator; iterating it on `φ₀ ≡ y₀` produces the article's sequence `φₖ`.

annotations.label: Convergence of Picard iterates to the solution

anchors.snippet: is convergent and that its limit is a solution

annotations.note: The generic Banach fixed-point convergence lemma `ContractingWith.tendsto_iterate_fixedPoint` specialises to give `φₖ → φ` for `φₖ = Γ^[k] φ₀` once `Γ` is shown to be a contraction (which Mathlib does inside `FunSpace.exists_isFixedPt_next`).

annotations.label: Picard iterates for y' = 1 + y² converge to tan(t)

anchor.snippet: computing the Taylor series expansion of our known solution

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Real.tan` and `Real.hasDerivAt_tan`, but the specific worked Picard-iterate calculation for `y' = 1 + y²` with `y(0)=0` is not formalized.

annotations.label: Non-uniqueness counter-example: dy/dt = a·y^(2/3)

anchors.snippet: By contrast for an equation in which the stationary point can be reached after a finite time

annotations.note: Counter-example showing necessity of the Lipschitz hypothesis; Mathlib formalizes the positive theorem (Picard–Lindelöf) but not this explicit non-uniqueness witness.

annotations.label: Picard operator on the space of continuous curves
annotations.note: Mathlib's `FunSpace.next` is the Picard operator acting on the `FunSpace` of Lipschitz curves from `Icc tmin tmax` to a closed ball; its underlying formula is `ODE.picard`.
annotations.proof_note: The article's domain is the space of continuous functions `I_a → B_b(y₀)`; Mathlib instead uses the smaller `FunSpace` of `L`-Lipschitz functions, which postpones the use of completeness until the contraction step.

annotations.label: Picard operator is a contraction (when a < 1/L)
annotations.note: Mathlib bounds `dist (next^[n] α) (next^[n] β)` by `(K|t-t₀|)ⁿ/n! · dist α β`; the article's case `n = 1` is the contraction estimate `‖Γφ₁−Γφ₂‖ ≤ aL · ‖φ₁−φ₂‖`.
annotations.proof_note: Mathlib never argues that `Γ` itself is a contraction; it goes directly to the stronger `Γⁿ` estimate so the interval length need not be shrunk below `1/L`.

annotations.label: Iterated-contraction estimate ‖Γᵐφ₁−Γᵐφ₂‖ ≤ Lᵐ|t−t₀|ᵐ/m! · ‖φ₁−φ₂‖
annotations.note: Exactly the inductive bound used by the article, with `K` (the Lipschitz constant of `f`) playing the role of `L`.

annotations.label: Corollary: if Tⁿ is a contraction then T has a unique fixed point
annotations.note: `ContractingWith.isFixedPt_fixedPoint_iterate` states that the fixed point of `f^[n]` is a fixed point of `f`; combined with `fixedPoint_unique'` this gives the article's corollary.

annotations.label: Solution interval depends only on the field's domain and bound, not on L

anchor.snippet: the interval of definition of the solution does not depend on the Lipschitz constant

annotations.note: In Mathlib `IsPicardLindelof` only requires `L · max(tmax−t₀, t₀−tmin) ≤ a − r` (with `L` the bound on `‖f‖` and `K` the Lipschitz constant), so the validity interval depends on the field's bound and domain, not on the Lipschitz constant.

annotations.label: Peano existence theorem
annotations.note: Existence of a solution to `y' = f(t,y)` under only continuity of `f` is not formalized in Mathlib's ODE library.

annotations.label: Three solutions of dy/dt = y^(1/3) with y(0) = 0

anchors.snippet: Indeed, rather than being unique, this equation has at least three solutions

annotations.note: Counter-example illustrating that Peano existence does not give uniqueness; not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Carathéodory's existence theorem
annotations.note: Existence of solutions under measurability + integrability hypotheses on `f` (Carathéodory conditions) is not formalized in Mathlib's ODE library.

annotations.label: Okamura's uniqueness theorem
annotations.note: Okamura's necessary-and-sufficient criterion for uniqueness of IVP solutions is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Global existence under a global Lipschitz hypothesis

anchor.snippet: if f is globally Lipschitz, then the local interval of existence of each solution can be extended to the entire real line

annotations.note: Mathlib has only local existence (`IsPicardLindelof.exists_eq_forall_mem_Icc_hasDerivWithinAt`); a dedicated statement giving an `IsIntegralCurve` on all of `ℝ` from a globally Lipschitz field is not yet in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Blow-up counter-example: dy/dt = y², y(0) = 1

anchor.snippet: y(t) = 1/(1-t), which is not defined at t = 1

annotations.note: Counter-example showing that locally-Lipschitz fields need not produce globally defined solutions; not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Integral curves of a smooth vector field on a compact manifold exist for all time

anchor.snippet: if f is a differentiable vector field defined over a domain which is a compact smooth manifold, then all its trajectories

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `IsIntegralCurve` in `Mathlib.Analysis.ODE.Basic` but does not yet have the manifold-level result that a smooth vector field on a compact manifold has complete (all-time) integral curves.
